Source_Species.reason: The study species is Populus trichocarpa, i.e. poplar.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Explicitly stated throughout the paper as Populus trichocarpa.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Methods states P. trichocarpa genotype Nisqually-1 was used for all experiments.

Applied_Species.reason: The transgenic experiments were performed in poplar.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species is explicitly P. trichocarpa.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods states genotype Nisqually-1 for all experiments.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods states the original 35S promoter was replaced by the xylem-specific promoter of Ptr4CL3.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results states monolignol biosynthetic genes are abundantly expressed in both fiber and vessel cells in stem-differentiating xylem.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Same transgenic/applied species context; abundant expression in fiber and vessel cells is stated.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper centers on monolignol/lignin biosynthesis within the phenylpropanoid pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: PAL is described as acting from phenylalanine into the monolignol pathway; this is the entry step.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number for PAL is provided in the paper.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Fig. 1/pathway description starts from phenylalanine as precursor.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper does not explicitly state trans-cinnamic acid as PAL product in the provided text, though it is standard biochemistry.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This specific reaction description is not explicitly stated in the paper text, though consistent with PAL function.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: PAL is the upstream entry step from phenylalanine into monolignol biosynthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The pathway leads to lignin built from H, G, and S monolignols.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Lignin is described as a major phenolic polymer.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Lignin is described in wood/secondary cell walls; stem-differentiating xylem and wood are the analyzed tissues.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper repeatedly states lignin determines wood properties important for timber, pulp, and biofuels.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: PAL supplies the precursor entry pool for monolignol/lignin biosynthesis.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: PAL downregulation is predicted as best single-family perturbation for wood quality; reduced lignin is linked to improved saccharification and C:L ratio, and PAL transgenics with low lignin are discussed.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are supported: broad PAL suppression ranges are shown; PAL affects lignin/wood properties through the integrative analysis; PAL, PAL+CCoAOMT, and PAL+C3H+CCoAOMT are highlighted in Fig. 10/results.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper identifies PAL as a strong engineering target, but does not explicitly call it rate-limiting.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Greenhouse growth, seasonal/environmental transcript variation, and saccharification with/without hot-water pretreatment are explicitly described.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: No PAL subcellular localization is stated in this paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: PAL family membership is implicit from gene naming, but aromatic amino acid lyase domain/family is not stated in the paper.

Omics_Data.reason: RNA-seq GSE78953, proteomics by PC-IDMS, model-derived fluxes, 2D NMR, and wood chemistry/phenomics are all explicitly described.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods are explicitly the core study design and analyses.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: Results explicitly highlight PAL as a strong target alone and in combinations for improved saccharification/C:L ratio and multi-trait engineering.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results states effects of changing an entire gene family are greater than individual genes, indicating redundancy.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Paper notes some monolignol gene perturbations affect development, but specifically says some low-lignin PAL transgenics retained growth similar to wildtype.
